translation: The Moon transferring the light and influence of the Lord of the ascendant to the Lord of the eighth, brings usually death: so also when the Lord of the eighth is in the ascendant, the Lord of the ascendant and the Moon being both afflicted: It also proves fatal when the Lord of the ascendant is unfortunate in the eighth, the Moon being then corrupted or very weak, and in no essential Dignity: the Lord of the ascendant being Subterranean, and in any aspect to the Lord of the eighth in the eighth, the Moon also then vitiated, show great peril of death, and usually by reason of the ill government of the sick party, or some error in his ordering and course in Physic: it's a powerful argument that the sick party will die, when at time of his first Question to his Physician, you find the Lord of the ascendant Combust in the ascendant.

author: William Lilly
title: Lord of Ascendant and 8th Unfortunate
text: The Lord of the afcendant and of the eighth unfortunate, prenote death.

author: William Lilly
title: Lord of 8th in 10th, Ascendant Lord Afflicted
text: The Lord of the eighth in the tenth houfe, and Lord of the afcendant in fourth, fixe or feventh, affli&ted of the malevolent Planets, argue death.

author: William Lilly
title: Strong Planet Lord of Hour and 8th
text: A Planet very ftrong, and placed in the afcendant, if he be Lord of the houre and of the eighth, portends death: if the Lord of the eighth be Retrograde, and in Square or Oppofition of the Moon, it fhewes death : The Lord of the eighth in the feventh, the Moon and Lord of the afcendant in cadent houfes, infe&ted with the ill afpects of Infortunes ; and more certaine, if one of the malevolents be Lord of the eighth, or pofited in the eighth ; some say, if the Moon be in conjunction with Saturn or Mars, the fickneffe will have little good thereby, nor will he escape, unleffe Saturn be Retrograde and Jupiter Direct:
translation: A Planet very strong, and placed in the ascendant, if he be Lord of the hour and of the eighth, portends death: if the Lord of the eighth be Retrograde, and in Square or Opposition of the Moon, it shows death: The Lord of the eighth in the seventh, the Moon and Lord of the ascendant in cadent houses, infected with the ill aspects of Infortunes; and more certain, if one of the malevolents be Lord of the eighth, or posited in the eighth; some say, if the Moon be in conjunction with Saturn or Mars, the sickness will have little good thereby, nor will he escape, unless Saturn be Retrograde and Jupiter Direct:

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant Lord with 8th Lord or Planet in 8th
text: When the Lord of the Afcendant is in conjunction with the Lord of the eighth, or in Square or Oppofition of a Planet pofited in that houfe, or in the Antifcion of the Lord of the eighth, without the benevolent Sextile or Trine of Jupiter ; and at the same time the Moon be anyway affli&ted, it's probable the fick will dye; but if the Lord of the afcendant be in reception with the Planet in the eighth, it's poffible he may avoyd death ; however, let him be affured a very long and grievous Difease he cannot : If the Moon be with Saturn or Mars without the affiftance of some good afpect from Jupiter and Venus ; and if Saturn be flow in motion, or is going Retrograde, it's so much the worse, and it's one argument the fick will dye at that time; if other teftimonies concurre, it's more certaine :
translation: When the Lord of the Ascendant is in conjunction with the Lord of the eighth, or in Square or Opposition of a Planet posited in that house, or in the Antiscion of the Lord of the eighth, without the benevolent Sextile or Trine of Jupiter; and at the same time the Moon be anyway afflicted, it's probable the sick will die; but if the Lord of the ascendant be in reception with the Planet in the eighth, it's possible he may avoid death; however, let him be assured a very long and grievous Disease he cannot: If the Moon be with Saturn or Mars without the assistance of some good aspect from Jupiter and Venus; and if Saturn be slow in motion, or is going Retrograde, it's so much the worse, and it's one argument the sick will die at that time; if other testimonies concur, it's more certain:

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant Lord in 7th, Fall, Cadent, Afflicted
text: The Lord of the afcendant in the feventh, in his Fall, or under the earth in the fourth or fixe, or in other Cadent houfes, afflicted by the malevolents, and the Lord of the eighth in the feventh, these are teftimonies of death:
translation: The Lord of the ascendant in the seventh, in his Fall, or under the earth in the fourth or sixth, or in other Cadent houses, afflicted by the malevolents, and the Lord of the eighth in the seventh, these are testimonies of death:

author: William Lilly
title: Malefic Near Ascendant or Fixed Star
text: A malevolent Planet neere to the degree afcending, or a violent fixed Starre, viz. Antares in the fourth 27, Lupus about the ninth of Libra, Palilicium in four 1, Caput Medufae, these prenote death;
translation: A malevolent Planet near to the degree ascending, or a violent fixed Star, viz. Antares in the fourth 27, Lupus about the ninth of Libra, Palilicium in four 1, Caput Medusae, these prenote death;

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant Lord Square/Opposite 6th or 12th Lord
text: The Lord of the afcendant in Square or Oppofition, in any bad configuration of the Lord of the fixt or twelfth, fhewes little hopes of recovery.
translation: The Lord of the ascendant in Square or Opposition, in any bad configuration of the Lord of the sixth or twelfth, shows little hopes of recovery.

author: William Lilly
title: Lights Afflicted by Saturn in Angles
text: Both the Lights affli&ted of Saturn in Angles, give teftimony of a tedious long fickneffe ; so doe both the Lights, being ill dignified and under the earth, fignifie the same: when as alfo the Sun from the beginning of the Difease fhall be corporally affli&ted, or by the Conjunction or Oppofition of Saturn or Mars impedited, or be in the perfe& Antifcion of a malignant Planet, or fhall apply and not separate, either death, or an extraordinary long fickneffe fucceeds:
translation: Both the Lights afflicted of Saturn in Angles, give testimony of a tedious long sickness; so do both the Lights, being ill dignified and under the earth, signify the same: when as also the Sun from the beginning of the Disease shall be corporally afflicted, or by the Conjunction or Opposition of Saturn or Mars impeded, or be in the perfect Antiscion of a malignant Planet, or shall apply and not separate, either death, or an extraordinary long sickness succeeds:

author: William Lilly
title: Moon with Retrograde/Combust Ascendant Lord
text: The Moon after the beginning of the Difease comming to Conjunction of the Lord of the afcendant, and he Retrograde or Combust, argues death, or a fharp difease, not eafily curable:
translation: The Moon after the beginning of the Disease coming to Conjunction of the Lord of the ascendant, and he Retrograde or Combust, argues death, or a sharp disease, not easily curable:

author: William Lilly
title: Saturn with 8th Lord, Moon with Mars
text: Saturn in Conjunction with the Lord of the eight, the Moon in the fourth with Mars, or Moon in the afcendant, and neere the degree afcending, are arguments of death: the Moon befieged by the Infortunes, or between Sun and Mars in the eighth houfe, and in Square, or in Oppofition, in Conjunction or Oppofition to Saturn or Mars, or in conjunction with the Pleiades in 24 Taurus, or other violent fixed Starres, argues death: the Moon being Lady of the fixt, or of the afcendant in Combustion, and the Lord of the eighth at the same time affli&ted by Mars, or ill afpect of Saturn or Mars, fhew death.
translation: Saturn in Conjunction with the Lord of the eighth, the Moon in the fourth with Mars, or Moon in the ascendant, and near the degree ascending, are arguments of death: the Moon besieged by the Infortunes, or between Sun and Mars in the eighth house, and in Square, or in Opposition, in Conjunction or Opposition to Saturn or Mars, or in conjunction with the Pleiades in 24 Taurus, or other violent fixed Stars, argues death: the Moon being Lady of the sixth, or of the ascendant in Combustion, and the Lord of the eighth at the same time afflicted by Mars, or ill aspect of Saturn or Mars, show death.

author: William Lilly
title: Dariot: Significator in Contrary Sign
text: The Significator of the Querent in a Signe contrary to his owne nature, as of being Lord of the afcendant, and naturally hot and dry, if he be in Cancer, which is cold and moyft; or if the Lord of the afcendant be in a Cadent houfe, chiefly in the fixe, he is fick.
translation: The Significator of the Querent in a Sign contrary to his own nature, as of being Lord of the ascendant, and naturally hot and dry, if he be in Cancer, which is cold and moist; or if the Lord of the ascendant be in a Cadent house, chiefly in the sixth, he is sick.

author: William Lilly
title: Dariot: Diurnal Planet Ill-Affected
text: A diurnall Planet being Significator; and he under the earth, ill affected, Combust, Retrograde, in his Fall or Detriment, weak, or in Termes of malevolents, or with violent fixed Starres, or besieged by the two Infortunes, these things happening, the party is fick: What was spoken of a diurnal Planet, muft be understood of a nocturnall one (confideratis confiderands.)
translation: A diurnal Planet being Significator; and he under the earth, ill affected, Combust, Retrograde, in his Fall or Detriment, weak, or in Terms of malevolents, or with violent fixed Stars, or besieged by the two Infortunes, these things happening, the party is sick: What was spoken of a diurnal Planet, must be understood of a nocturnal one (considering what needs to be considered).

author: William Lilly
title: Lilly: Querent Not Sick Indicators
text: If the afcendant were not affli&ted, or the Lord thereof out of his effentiall Dignities, or in any evill afpect of Saturn or Mars, or Lord of the fixt. Or if no Planet affli&ted the fixt houfe by prefence, or that the Moon were not affli&ted in the eighth or twelfth ; or if I found Jupiter or Venus or Sun in the afcendant, or the Moon in the fixt, or the Moon and Lord of the afcendant in any good afpect, or Jupiter or Venus cafting a Trine or Sextile to the cufp of the afcendant or fixe houfe, I would directly acquaint the party they were not fick, or that no fickneffe would fucced upon this Quere, but that their miftruft of a fickneffe was grounded upon some fudden diftemper of Body, which would prefently be rectified.
translation: If the ascendant were not afflicted, or the Lord thereof out of his essential Dignities, or in any evil aspect of Saturn or Mars, or Lord of the sixth. Or if no Planet afflicted the sixth house by presence, or that the Moon were not afflicted in the eighth or twelfth; or if I found Jupiter or Venus or Sun in the ascendant, or the Moon in the sixth, or the Moon and Lord of the ascendant in any good aspect, or Jupiter or Venus casting a Trine or Sextile to the cusp of the ascendant or sixth house, I would directly acquaint the party they were not sick, or that no sickness would succeed upon this Query, but that their mistrust of a sickness was grounded upon some sudden distemper of Body, which would presently be rectified.

author: William Lilly
title: Inward Cause of Disease
text: The inward caufe and condition of the Difease we require from the ill difpofition of the Significator, in Signe; Houfe and place of Heaven, his good or ill configuration with the malevolent Planets : where generally obferve, any Planet may in this cafe be malignant, if he be Lord of the eighth, twelfth or fixe, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Significator and Body Part Pained
text: If the Significator be a masculine Planet and above the earth, the right side of the Sicke is pained; and if the Significator be in few degrees of the Signe, the upper part of that Member is pained or grieved ; if the Significator be in the middle of the Signe, the middle part of the Member is diftreffed, and so the lower part of the Member, when the fignificator poffeffeth the lower degrees of the Sign.

author: William Lilly
title: Disease in Body, Mind, or Both
text: You muft understand in the firft place, that the Signe afcending the Moon and the Lord of the houfe wherein the Sun is, doe fhew the Spirit of Man, and that the Lord of the Afcendant, the Planet who is difpofitoer of the Moon, doth denote both the externall and internall Members. Wherefore in giving judgement herein, you may confider if the Afcendant Sun and Moon be all vitiated or affli&ted, the Difease is then through the whole Body, or no place is free: but if those Planets who difpose of the Sun and Moon, or he that is Lord of the Afcendant, or two of them at leaft be affli&ted, the Difease is in the Spirits together with some indifpofition of Minde; the reason hereof is, because the Lord of the Afcendant and Difpofitor of the Moon are properly the Significators of the Animal faculties and infirmities in Man, or which may chance unto him; as deprivation of Sence, Madneffe, Frenzie, Melancholly, &c. If the Afcendant, the Moon and Lord of the Houfe of the Sun are all or but two of them impedited, the infirmity refis in the Minde but not in the Body. If the Afcendant and the Moon be both unfortunate, and the Lord of the Afcendant and Difpofitor of the Moon free, the indifpofition is in the Minde and not in the Body.
translation: You must understand in the first place, that the Sign ascending, the Moon and the Lord of the house wherein the Sun is, do show the Spirit of Man, and that the Lord of the Ascendant, the Planet who is dispositor of the Moon, doth denote both the external and internal Members. Wherefore in giving judgment herein, you may consider if the Ascendant, Sun and Moon be all vitiated or afflicted, the Disease is then through the whole Body, or no place is free: but if those Planets who dispose of the Sun and Moon, or he that is Lord of the Ascendant, or two of them at least be afflicted, the Disease is in the Spirits together with some indisposition of Mind; the reason hereof is, because the Lord of the Ascendant and Dispositor of the Moon are properly the Significators of the Animal faculties and infirmities in Man, or which may chance unto him; as deprivation of Sense, Madness, Frenzy, Melancholy, etc. If the Ascendant, the Moon and Lord of the House of the Sun are all or but two of them impeded, the infirmity rests in the Mind but not in the Body. If the Ascendant and the Moon be both unfortunate, and the Lord of the Ascendant and Dispositor of the Moon free, the indisposition is in the Mind and not in the Body.

author: William Lilly
title: Saturn and Mental Affliction
text: This, generall rule many Aftrologians obferve, viz. that Saturn naturally forefhewes or caufeth Melancholly, all manner of diftempers from Melancholly, and by confequence the disturbed Minde ; wherefore wherefoever you finde Saturn Lord of the Afcendant or of the eighth, or twelith Houfe, or fixt, or if the Moon separate from him, or if Mars be in the fixt houfe, or in the Afcendant, or in Conjunction, or Square of the Lord of the Afcendant, the fick-party labours with some affli&tion of Minde, or with some vexatious care wherewith his minde is much troubled ; now if the contrary hereof the Lord of the Houfe of the Moon and of the Ascendant are unfortunate by the Sun, or Combust, or under his beames, the infirmity is Bodily.
translation: This, general rule many Astrologians observe, viz. that Saturn naturally foreshows or causes Melancholy, all manner of distempers from Melancholy, and by consequence the disturbed Mind; wherefore wheresoever you find Saturn Lord of the Ascendant or of the eighth, or twelfth House, or sixth, or if the Moon separate from him, or if Mars be in the sixth house, or in the Ascendant, or in Conjunction, or Square of the Lord of the Ascendant, the sick-party labors with some affliction of Mind, or with some vexatious care wherewith his mind is much troubled; now if the contrary hereof the Lord of the House of the Moon and of the Ascendant are unfortunate by the Sun, or Combust, or under his beams, the infirmity is Bodily.

author: William Lilly
title: Dispositors Afflicted, Mind Over Body
text: If that Planet who rules the Signe wherein the Lord of the Afcendant is in, and he who is Difpofitor of the Moone be infortunate in their fall, detriment or otherwayes very much affli&ted, the Difease raignes more in the Minde then in the Body.
translation: If that Planet who rules the Sign wherein the Lord of the Ascendant is in, and he who is Dispositor of the Moon be infortunate in their fall, detriment or other ways very much afflicted, the Disease reigns more in the Mind than in the Body.

author: William Lilly
title: Planet in Ascendant or Moon's House Oppressed
text: If a Planet in the Afcendant, or the Afcendant, or if the Lord of the Houfe of the Moone be oppreffed in the twelfth by a Square or Oppofition of Mars, the Difease is both in Body and Minde.
translation: If a Planet in the Ascendant, or the Ascendant, or if the Lord of the House of the Moon be oppressed in the twelfth by a Square or Opposition of Mars, the Disease is both in Body and Mind.

author: William Lilly
title: Malefic Beholding Ascendant, Not Moon
text: A Planet being by nature malevolent, beholding the Afcendant and not the Moone, and together with this, if the Lord of the Signe where the Sun is be afflicted, the party is grieved in Minde, but not fick in his Body. Alfo, if the degree afcending and degree of that Signe wherein the Moon is be more affli&ted then the Lords of those Signes, the Difease ranges more in the Minde then Body, and so the contrary when the Lords are more affli&ted then the parts of the Signes before mentioned.
translation: A Planet being by nature malevolent, beholding the Ascendant and not the Moon, and together with this, if the Lord of the Sign where the Sun is be afflicted, the party is grieved in Mind, but not sick in his Body. Also, if the degree ascending and degree of that Sign wherein the Moon is be more afflicted than the Lords of those Signs, the Disease ranges more in the Mind than Body, and so the contrary when the Lords are more afflicted than the parts of the Signs before mentioned.

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant Lord/Sun in Exaltation, Moon's Dispositor Afflicted
text: If the Lord of the Afcendant and the Sun be in their exaltations, and the difpofitor of the Moon in his detriment or fall, &c. the Difease raignes in the Body, not in the Minde.
translation: If the Lord of the Ascendant and the Sun be in their exaltations, and the dispositor of the Moon in his detriment or fall, etc. the Disease reigns in the Body, not in the Mind.

author: William Lilly
title: Lords of Lights Afflicted, Ascendant Degree Square Moon
text: When the Lords of the places of the Moon and of the Sun be in their detriments, fals, or Peregrine, Retrograde, Combust, and the degree afcending in Square of the Moon ; and free from the ill afpects of Saturn and Mars, then is the Patient vexed with a tormented Soule.
translation: When the Lords of the places of the Moon and of the Sun be in their detriments, falls, or Peregrine, Retrograde, Combust, and the degree ascending in Square of the Moon; and free from the ill aspects of Saturn and Mars, then is the Patient vexed with a tormented Soul.

author: William Lilly
title: Moon, Ascendant Lord, 12th Lord as Significators
text: Ufually when the Moon the Lord of the Afcendant, or houre, or of the twelfth houfe are fignificators of the party inquiring, these fhew a Minde vexed with haughtineffe, vaine-glory, felfe-conceitedneffe, Pride, &c.
translation: Usually when the Moon, the Lord of the Ascendant, or hour, or of the twelfth house are significators of the party inquiring, these show a Mind vexed with haughtiness, vain-glory, self-conceitedness, Pride, etc.

author: William Lilly
title: Venus as Significator
text: Venus argues luxury, a lafcivious defire to Women, wherwith both Body and Minde are difturbed.

author: William Lilly
title: Mars as Significator Afflicted
text: Mars fhewes doating fancies, and fearfull imaginations, wherefoever you finde him a Significator and afflicted : as alfo, that he is ftirred to miftruft upon, vaine feares, his owne jealous fancies, or upon some flying reports.

author: William Lilly
title: Birth Degrees and Eclipses/Conjunctions
text: Over and above the many Directions formerly prefcribed, you muft well confider whether the degrees wherein the Lord of the Afcendant, the Sun or Moon at time of the Birth (if you have the Patients Nativity) fall to be the degrees of a Signe wherein a prefent Eclipfe is, at time of the fickneffe or neere it, or of some eminent great Conjun&tion ; for I muft tell you, these are all unfortunate.
translation: Over and above the many Directions formerly prescribed, you must well consider whether the degrees wherein the Lord of the Ascendant, the Sun or Moon at time of the Birth (if you have the Patient's Nativity) fall to be the degrees of a Sign wherein a present Eclipse is, at time of the sickness or near it, or of some eminent great Conjunction; for I must tell you, these are all unfortunate.

author: William Lilly
title: Eclipse/Conjunction Sign in Angles
text: The figne of the Eclips or of a great Conjunction threatning evill, or the Signe of the eighth Houfe of the yeerly revolution of the World, falling in any of the Angles of the Nativity, efpecially in the afcendant, proves very dangerous.

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant at Sickness with Natal Infortune
text: When a Signe afcends upon the firft falling fick, or demand of the Patient, wherein an Infortune was in the Nativity, it moft fearfully torments the fick party, viz. it fhewes he fhall have a hard fit of Sickneffe:

author: William Lilly
title: Moon's Conjunction with Sun
text: The Conjunction of the Moon with the Sun is a very ill figne, when there's not above six degrees diftance betwixt them, and the Moon not-yet paffed by the Sun, that is, not having been yet in Conjunction with him: however, upon the Sun and Moon, their being so in Conjunction in Aries or Libra or Cancer or Capricorn, this misfortune is leffened ; when the Moon is twelve degrees from the Sun, fhe fhewes little danger.
translation: The Conjunction of the Moon with the Sun is a very ill sign, when there's not above six degrees distance betwixt them, and the Moon not yet passed by the Sun, that is, not having been yet in Conjunction with him: however, upon the Sun and Moon, their being so in Conjunction in Aries or Libra or Cancer or Capricorn, this misfortune is lessened; when the Moon is twelve degrees from the Sun, she shows little danger.

author: William Lilly
title: Crisis Points by Moon's Motion
text: To find the true Cryfis, you muft as neere as can be obtained, get the hour wherein the Patient firft took his Bed ; which if it cannot be had, then take the hour when firft Judgment was required of the Phyfitian, and rectifie the Moon her motion to that very hour; if the Difease be not chronick, but acute, you fhall find great alteration in the Difease and party infirmed, meet upon those times when the Moon comes to be diftant from that her firft place, 45. degrees ; so alfo when fhe is 90. degrees from that place; and againe, when diftant 135. for difcovering whether the Cryfis will be good or ill, you muft note what Planet fhe is in afpect withall at those times, whether with a friendly Planet or an Infortune, If fhe be in a good afpect at those times with a benevolent Planet, it doth promise ease, and a better condition in the Difease; but if fhe then meet with an ill afpect of the Lord of the eighth or fixth, the Patient will be worse, his paine encreases, and the Medicine doe little good.
translation: To find the true Crisis, you must as near as can be obtained, get the hour wherein the Patient first took his Bed; which if it cannot be had, then take the hour when first Judgment was required of the Physician, and rectify the Moon her motion to that very hour; if the Disease be not chronic, but acute, you shall find great alteration in the Disease and party infirm, meet upon those times when the Moon comes to be distant from that her first place, 45 degrees; so also when she is 90 degrees from that place; and again, when distant 135. for discovering whether the Crisis will be good or ill, you must note what Planet she is in aspect withal at those times, whether with a friendly Planet or an Infortune, If she be in a good aspect at those times with a benevolent Planet, it doth promise ease, and a better condition in the Disease; but if she then meet with an ill aspect of the Lord of the eighth or sixth, the Patient will be worse, his pain increases, and the Medicine do little good.

author: William Lilly
title: Moon's Aspects and Disease Outcome
text: I ufually obferve, and I doe not remember that I have failed, viz. that as oft as the Moon came to Conjunction, Square or Oppofition of that Planet who did any wayes either affli& the afcendant, the Lord of the afcendant or the Moon, or when fhe came to the like afpect of the Lord of the fixe, or any Planet that was pofited in the fixe; I say, then I did ever obferve the Patient to be much diftempered, the Difease high, and Medicines given about those times to work little or no good effe&; when I obferved the Moon to come to a Trine or Sextile of the Lord of the afcendant, or Lord of the eleventh, or Lord of the ninth or tenth, I use to pronounce to the Infirmed, comfort, and some relaxation or an intervall of ease; so alfo, when the Lord of the afcendant came to any good afpect of the Sun (if he had not power or dominion in the disease) I found the Patient's mind much enlightned.
translation: I usually observe, and I do not remember that I have failed, viz. that as oft as the Moon came to Conjunction, Square or Opposition of that Planet who did any ways either afflict the ascendant, the Lord of the ascendant or the Moon, or when she came to the like aspect of the Lord of the sixth, or any Planet that was posited in the sixth; I say, then I did ever observe the Patient to be much distempered, the Disease high, and Medicines given about those times to work little or no good effect; when I observed the Moon to come to a Trine or Sextile of the Lord of the ascendant, or Lord of the eleventh, or Lord of the ninth or tenth, I use to pronounce to the Infirm, comfort, and some relaxation or an interval of ease; so also, when the Lord of the ascendant came to any good aspect of the Sun (if he had not power or dominion in the disease) I found the Patient's mind much enlightened.

author: William Lilly
title: Timing Recovery by Aspect Degrees
text: When I find, that by God's bleffing the fick party fhall recover, and it be demanded, When or about what time it is like to be? I ufually obferve, who is the Lord of the afcendant, and which of the benevolent Planets he is in afpect with, and how many degrees there are diftant betwixt them, in what houfe they both are in, viz. whether in Angles, Succedant or Cadent, what Signes they poffeffe, whether Moveable, Fixed or Common, and according to difcretion and quality of the Difease, so I frame my measure of time; yet ordinarily if the afpect be in moveable Signes, I judge, in so many dayes the party will amend, the more certainly, I determine, if the Significators be fwift in motion, angular. If the application be in common Signes, I neither judge moneths, weeks or dayes, but according as I can with difcretion frame my judgement, having firft obferved the nature of the Difease, and poffibility of determining in such or such a time, the Ancients did say : Moveable Signes fhew Dayes. Common Signes, Weeks or Moneths. Fixed Signes, Moneths or Yeers. Angles are equivalent to moveable Signes. Succedant to common Signes. Cadent to fixed Signes.
translation: When I find, that by God's blessing the sick party shall recover, and it be demanded, When or about what time it is like to be? I usually observe, who is the Lord of the ascendant, and which of the benevolent Planets he is in aspect with, and how many degrees there are distant betwixt them, in what house they both are in, viz. whether in Angles, Succedant or Cadent, what Signs they possess, whether Moveable, Fixed or Common, and according to discretion and quality of the Disease, so I frame my measure of time; yet ordinarily if the aspect be in moveable Signs, I judge, in so many days the party will amend, the more certainly, I determine, if the Significators be swift in motion, angular. If the application be in common Signs, I neither judge months, weeks or days, but according as I can with discretion frame my judgment, having first observed the nature of the Disease, and possibility of determining in such or such a time, the Ancients did say: Moveable Signs show Days. Common Signs, Weeks or Months. Fixed Signs, Months or Years. Angles are equivalent to moveable Signs. Succedant to common Signs. Cadent to fixed Signs.

author: William Lilly
title: Lord of Ascendant Changing Sign
text: I many times find, when the Lord of the afcendant moves out of the Signe he is in at the time of the Queftion, and hath effentiall Dignities in the Signe he is going into, the party recovers: then, or fenfibly feels an alteration for good in himfelfe; and so if the later degrees of a Signe are on the cufp of the fixt, viz. if I find 28. degrees, and the Signe common, I say, the Difease will vary in leffe time then two weeks:
translation: I many times find, when the Lord of the ascendant moves out of the Sign he is in at the time of the Question, and hath essential Dignities in the Sign he is going into, the party recovers: then, or sensibly feels an alteration for good in himself; and so if the later degrees of a Sign are on the cusp of the sixth, viz. if I find 28 degrees, and the Sign common, I say, the Disease will vary in less time than two weeks:

author: William Lilly
title: Hermes: Planetary Influence on Body
text: The heavenly Rayes or Influences proceeding and emitted from the seven Planets are multiplied and difpersed into the feverall members of man, even whileft the conception in the Mothers Wombe, doth firft begin to cleave together: neither verily doth it happen otherwayes when the Child firft sees the light of this world, but even according to the pofition of the twelve Signes of Heaven, so doe we affigne the Head to the Signe Aries.

author: William Lilly
title: Hermes: Planets and Sensitive Parts
text: The Senfitive Parts or Inftruments of Mans Body are thus attributed to the feven Planets. The right Eye to the Sun, the left to the Moon. The fenfe of Hearing and Eares to Saturn. The Braine to Jupiter. Blood to Mars. Smelling and Tasting to Venus. The Tongue, the Weefell-pipe of a mans Throat or Lungs pipe to Mercury.

author: William Lilly
title: Hermes: Afflicted Planet and Body Defect
text: That member fuffers a defect or imperfe&tion; of which either at conception or birth an afflicted Planet had dominion, or did fignifie the same: There are alfo in Man foure more principall and generall parts ; the Head, the Breaft, the Hands, and Feete. If the Planet who governeth any of those principall parts be unfortunate and ill affe&ted, either at the time of Conception or Birth, the same Planet affli&teth or disfigureth all those parts so attributed unto himfelfe, or some particular or principall part of those members. As when the Sun or Moone be ill difpofed or vitiated, either the one or both, the eyes receive prejudice; if Saturn the eares, teeth, or fence of hearing. When Mercury is oppreffed, we finde a defect in the tongue, or ftammering in fpeech: And in the same manner we may apprehend, whether any part in the Breaft, Lungs, Liver, Spleen, Heart, or any of the inteftine or inwards of the body be corrupted and infe&ted, radically from the Birth or Conception.
translation: That member suffers a defect or imperfection; of which either at conception or birth an afflicted Planet had dominion, or did signify the same: There are also in Man four more principal and general parts; the Head, the Breast, the Hands, and Feet. If the Planet who governeth any of those principal parts be unfortunate and ill affected, either at the time of Conception or Birth, the same Planet afflicteth or disfigureth all those parts so attributed unto himself, or some particular or principal part of those members. As when the Sun or Moon be ill disposed or vitiated, either the one or both, the eyes receive prejudice; if Saturn the ears, teeth, or sense of hearing. When Mercury is oppressed, we find a defect in the tongue, or stammering in speech: And in the same manner we may apprehend, whether any part in the Breast, Lungs, Liver, Spleen, Heart, or any of the intestine or inwards of the body be corrupted and infected, radically from the Birth or Conception.

author: William Lilly
title: Infortune in 11th House (Officers)
text: If an Infortune be in the eleventh, the Conductor or Conductors may be men of fidelity, and assured Friends and Well-willers to the cause they undertake, but they are unexpert, and not fit to undertake such a weighty employment in hand, as they nothing understand the stratagems of War, whereby the whole cause is like to suffer.

author: William Lilly
title: Fortune or Infortune in 12th House (Adversary)
text: If a Fortune be in the twelfth house, those against whom the Army is to go, are well provided, and resolved to defend themselves; they agree well, fear nothing, will stand it out to the last: but if an Infortune be there, they suspect their own abilities, are not capable of resisting, disagree amongst themselves, fear surprisal every moment: It is, notwithstanding, ever considerable, that if Saturn be in the twelfth house, the querent may justly suspect treachery, and indeed you have just cause to fear the same if Mars be in the twelfth.

author: William Lilly
title: Will Armies Fight?
text: Behold herein the ascendant and his Lord, the Moon and Lord of the seventh, see if they be corporally joined in any angle, then it seems the Armies will fight: if there be no conjunction of the Lord of the ascendant and seventh, see if they behold one another by trine or sextile, they will also then fight: if this happen not, see if any Planet transfer the light of one to the other by a trine or sextile aspect, with or without Reception; if such an aspect be, there will be a fight betwixt them: but if the more ponderous of the two receive that Planet who transfers their light, no fight will be, but all things will be composed lightly.

author: William Lilly
title: Judging a Nominated Adversary
text: If any be nominated, require judgment from the seventh house and Lord thereof: if the Lord of the seventh aspect the Lord of the ascendant, with conjunction or opposition, or be in like aspect with the Moon, it's then very probable, the party enquired after doth envy the Querent, and wishes him no good: if the aspect be separated, they have lately been in some contest, or some difference hath been betwixt them; but if they are then applying to a conjunction or opposition, the enmity, difference or controversie is approaching, is not yet over, will grow to a greater height then now it is, and the party enquired after, doth what in him lies to thwart and cross the occasions of the querent.

author: William Lilly
title: Adversary's Aversion and Enmity
text: In like manner, consider if the Lord of the seventh be in the twelfth from the ascendant, or in the twelfth sign from the place wherein the Lord of the ascendant is in, or from the place wherein the Moon is, or if the Lord of the seventh be in conjunction with any Planet, or in any aspect with a Planet who is in square or opposition to the Lord of the ascendant or the Moon, without Reception, then the Quesited, or man or Woman nominated, is averse, and an enemy, to the Querent, but if it be not so, then he or she enquired after is no enemy.

author: William Lilly
title: General Enemies (12th House)
text: If the Question be absolute, (as thus) Whether have I enemies : yea or no? you must require judgment herein from the twelfth house, and see if the Lord of that house be in conjunction or opposition to the Moon with or without Reception; if so, then he hath enemies that watch for an opportunity against him, but they do all things clandestinely and cunningly, and desire to play their part when they can do it without noise or rumour of evil, that so they may still go under the notion of Friends, when as in truth they are treacherous, false and deceitful: Consider also where and in what house the Lord of the twelfth is, say confidently such people, men or women of such a quality or condition, are the Querent's adversaries.

author: William Lilly
title: Quantity of Enemies or Want
text: Many Planets in the seventh, denotes many enemies; many Planets in the second, much want of money, if they are ill dignified, &c. and so do in all the rest, observing how many Planets there are in the seventh, and of what houses they are Lords of, or from the houses whereof they are Lords, from thence do you require the quality of the people who are enemies, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Reconciliation by Aspect
text: Remembering, that the sextile aspect shows envy and malice, yet possible to be reconciled, trine aspect without Reception, never, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Saturnine Temperament
text: I judged the Gentleman to be sad, angry, much discontented, and scorning his former flights, (as ever all Saturnine people do ;)

author: William Lilly
title: Marriage Outcome by Application & Reception
text: In Astrologie, the true reason of this performance is no more then, first, an application of the two Significators to a conjunction Viz. the Lord of the seventh and first: Next, the application of the Moon to the Lord of the ascendant, though by square, yet with Reception, was another small argument; but conjunction of Saturn Lord of the seventh, receiving his virtue which Venus did render unto him, and he again transferred to the Sun Lord of the ascendant, he, viz. Jupiter, meeting with no manner of prohibition, abscission or frustration until his perfect conjunction with the Sun, so that no difficulty did afterwards intervene.

author: William Lilly
title: Interposition of Influence
text: I told her she should not fear his marrying of Mars: Which judgment I gave, by reason Mars was nearer Mars than Saturn, and so interposed his influence, or kept off Saturn.

author: William Lilly
title: Significator of a Fugitive Servant
text: The Significator of the Servant was Mercury peculiarly in this Figure, although many times Venus shall signifie a fugitive Servant.

author: William Lilly
title: Judging Fugitive's Direction
text: Usually I find, that all Fugitives go by the Moon, and as she varies her Sign, so the Fugitive wavers and shifts in his flight, and declines more or less to East, West, North or South: but when the Question is demanded, then without doubt you must consider the strength both of the Significator and the Moon; and judge by the stronger; if both be equivalent in Fortitudes, judge either by the Significator, if he best personate the Fugitive, or by the Moon, if she most resemble him; with relation to either of them that comes nearest in aspect to the cusp of the house, from whence signification is taken.

author: William Lilly
title: Significator of a Thief (Master Allen)
text: It was the received opinion of Master Allen of Oxford, a man excellently versed in Astrologie; that the true Significator of a Thief is that Planet who is in an angle or second house, and beholds the seventh house: if no peregrine Planet be in an angle or the second house, then the Lord of the seventh shall be Significator of the Thief, if he behold the seventh house: otherwise that Planet to whom the Moon applies, if he behold the seventh house; the rather, if the Moon separate from the Lord of the ascendant. And he saith further, that peregrine Planet in what angle soever; shall not be Significator of the Thief, unless he behold the seventh house, or have any dignity in the degree of the seventh: yet if one and the same Planet be Lord of the hour and of the ascendant, he shall signify the Thief, though he behold not the ascendant.

author: William Lilly
title: Lilly's Thief Significator (Peregrine)
text: The truth is, I have ever found that if a peregrine Planet were in the ascendant, he was Significator of the Thief: next to the ascendant, I preferred the angle of the South, then the West angle, then the fourth house, last of all the second; many peregrine Planets in angles, many are or may be suspected, justly if they are in conjunction, opposition or trine; not consenting, if in square or sextile: ever prefer that peregrine Planet for your Significator who is nearest to the cusp of the angle he.

author: William Lilly
title: Importance of Accurate Birth Time
text: He that would judge upon the future actions and contingencies depending upon the influences of heaven in a Nativity, it is necessary that he have in the first place the place of the Planets, viz. their Motions exactly, calculated, rectified, and fitted for judgement, according to the Moderne and best approved rules of Art; that is, he ought first to set his Figure according to the estimative time given unto him; and then consider whether that be the true time of Birth yea or not, lest he be deceived either wholly in the Signe ascending, or by a fallacious and uncertaine houre mistake many degrees thereof, viz. either in having few or no degrees, or the latter part of any Signe ascending; by which errour no certaine or rationall judgement can be given, either of the Complexion, forme, constitution or fortune of the Native.

author: William Lilly
title: Trutine of Hermes (Conception)
text: The first way then of rectifying a Nativity, and reducing it to that moment of time when first the Infant was seperated from his Mother, and received the breath or ayre of this world, was by the Trutine or Scrutiny of Hermes (one of the wisest of all mortall men, and as ancient as Moyfes) and this way is farre more ancient then the Animador of Ptolomy, allowed by Ptolomy himselfe in his 51. Centiloquium (if that be his) as I undoubtedly conceive it is; his words are: What Signe the Moon is in at time of the birth, make that very Signe the ascendant at Conception; and what Signe the Moone is in when the Childe is conceived, make that Signe, or the opposite unto it the Signe ascending at the Birth, &c. For Hermes was of this opinion, that the very degree of the same Signe wherein the Moon was at the conception of the Childe, should be the true degree of the ascendant at the Birth.

author: William Lilly
title: Lilly's Critique of Hermes' Trutine
text: This manner of verification, though it is of great use and much experience, will not in many examples hold firme not to a degree two or three, all the use I ever have or could make of it, was, that when an uncertaine time was given me, or the time mistaken by an houre or two, it would helpe me to the Signe ascending, but rarely to the degree ascending or neer unto it; yet doe I know Junctine doth insist much upon it, and produces many examples verified by it, which did concurre with the Scheames of Heaven corrected by Accidents; many Authors also had a good opinion of it as well as he, viz. Scovoner, Pontanus, Sir Christopher Heydons, and others.

author: William Lilly
title: Determine Birth Day Number
text: Consider whether the yeer of your Birth be Common or Bissextill, or the yeer after, &c. and the day of birth is, entring the whole Moneths, adding thereunto the day of the Month, wherein the birth is.

author: William Lilly
title: Calculate Conception Day
text: The number of the Mansion of the Childe in its Mothers wombe, is to be substratted from the day of the birth; and, if substraction cannot be made other wayes, then adde the dayes of the whole yeer, viz. 365. or 366. to the day of the birth, and what remaines is the number of dayes wherein the conception was.

author: William Lilly
title: Find Conception Month and Day
text: With the dayes remaining, enter the Table of Moneths, and you shall find the Moneth and day of the Moneth, wherein the Conception was.

author: William Lilly
title: Verify Conception Day by Moon
text: Consider the place of the Moon the day of Conception at noones-time, which if she be not distant from the estimative Angle or ascendant of the Nativity above 13. degrees, the day found out is the day of Conception; but if she is more remote, you may imagine either the good aspects of the fortunes put the Birth, forward, or the untoward aspects of the infortunes retarded it.

author: William Lilly
title: Rectification by Accidents
text: This Nativity is precisely rectified by Accidents, both by those depending upon the Directions of Medium Celi, and of the ascendant to their respective Promittors.

author: William Lilly
title: Hour of Conception (Sun/Moon Ascensions)
text: Some have delivered a way to find out the hour of Conception, but I hold it a matter too nice fully at this time to be handled, nor give I any credit unto it: yet it's thus, You must take the right ascention of the Sun for the Noon of the day of Conception, deduced from Virgo, in what Signe soever Sun is: you must take the oblique ascention at the day of birth, of the Moon under the elevation of the Pole where the Birth is; substract the Sun his right ascention from the oblique of the Moon, what remains convert into time, and those houres shew the time of conception.

author: William Lilly
title: Hour of Conception (Sun/Moon Houses)
text: Or thus: Take the time from Noon in the Table of houses, adhering to the 10. house, over against the degrees of the Sun in the Signe he is in at the conception. Take the time from Noon over against the place of the Moon in the birth under the Ascendant. Substract the houres corresponding to the place of the Sun in the 10. house, from those answering the place of the Moon in the ascendant, by adding 24. houres, if need be; what remaines, is supposed to be the time of the houre of Conception.

author: William Lilly
title: Ptolomy's Animador Rectification
text: When you have erected your Scheame of heaven as neer as you can to the true estimative time, Ptolomy directs you to consider diligently the degree of the Signe wherein the last new Moone was before the Birth, or if it was a full Moon, the degree of that Signe wherein either of the lights that was above the earth was in. See what Planet in your Scheame hath most dignities, viz. essentiall in that degree; and if the degrees he is in be neerer to the degrees of the cuspe of the ascendant then to the cuspe of the Mid-heaven, place so many degrees ascending as the Planet is in the Signe who rules the degree wherein either the new Moon or full Moon was; but if his degrees be neerer the Mid-heaven then the ascendant, make the degrees of the Mid-heaven the same his are, and so vary your former figure according to either of those Angles; but if it happen sometimes two Planets have equall dignities in the degree aforesaid, accept of him who is neereest in degrees to the Ascendant, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Lilly's Judgment on Animador
text: Though our Nativity was rectified by accidents, and so needs not this way of rectification, yet for illustration thereof we will examine whether the verification hereof by Animador will concurre with what is verified by accidents; for the estimative time given me at first did not differ from the true and corrected above one degree in the ascendant, &c. Upon the 15. of Septemb. 1616. being Sunday there was an Eclipse of the Moon, or a full Moon foure dayes before the birth, and it was about eleven of clock in the day time, the Sun being in 2. degr. 32. min. of Virgo, and then above the earth, therefore I examine what Planet hath most dignities in that degree: if you looke into the Table of Essentiall dignities page 104. you shall finde Moon by reason of his exaltation and triplicity in the Signe and terme in that degree wherein the Sun is, that he is principall roler of that full Moon: if we examine the degree wherein he is in our Figure, we shall find him in 9 Virgo, which being neerer to the degree Ascending then of Mid-heaven, the Ascendant by this correction ought to have been the ninth of Virgo and 2. min. but had we accepted of Jupiter to have most dignities, as some would have done, you may then see a strange concurrence. I have onely delivered the way of this manner of emendation of the Horoscoope by that Method which is called Animador, but neither the Trutine of Hermes, or this, are of so sure foundation, as that Correction which is performed by Accidents.

author: William Lilly
title: Malefic Aspect to Ascendant or Moon
text: The quality of the Significators and Signs wherein they are placed are to be orderly examined; wherein you must not forget, that if either h or ¢ behold the ascendant or the ) with a malevolent aspect, they intermix their intemperate qualities to the temperature of the body, yea, albeit all other testimonies concur very well.

author: William Lilly
title: Moon's Qualities by Phase and Position
text: The ) From ¢ to first Quarter, Occidental, Dry. Hot and moist. Oriental, Hot and moist. From thence unto the Full, Occidental, Moist. Hot and dry. Oriental, Hot and Dry. From full ) to her last quarter: Occidental, Dry. Cold and dry. Oriental, Hot and moist. From last quarter to new D, Cold and moist. Oriental, Hot. is as % and @ as h and ¢. ) Occidental, Moist. Occidental, Dry.

author: William Lilly
title: Sun's Qualities by Season
text: The Sun is considered according to the Quarter of the Year. Spring, Hot and moist. Summer, Hot and dry. Autumn, Cold and dry. Winter, Cold and moist.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Moon's Terms
text: If the ) varies her Term or aspect, and doth meet with another of another quality, then do his Manners vary, and he assumes the conditions of that Planet to whose Terms or aspect she is directed, viz. if the ) comes to the Terms or aspect of 2, the Native is Cheerful; to the Terms or aspect of ¥, Discreet, Modest, Religious; to the Terms of ¢, Angry, Cholerick, Quarrelsome; to Terms or aspect of h, Grave, Melancholly, Sullen, full of Fears, Laborious, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Planet with Mercury or Moon
text: No Planet posited in the ascendant, observe what Planet is joined to ) or ¥, judge the manners of the Native to assimilate with the nature of that Planet. If the Planet be joined to ) and ¥ both, it’s as much as if there were many Planets in the Horoscope, for they signify discrepancy in manners; but yet those signified by the most powerful Planet shall continue longest, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Lord of Ascendant
text: No Planet in the ascendant, or joined to Mercury or Luna, then take the Lord of the ascendant, according to his nature, be it good or ill, and to judge of the manners; but so, as his Dispositor behold him with some aspect. If no Planet aspect him, have recourse to that Planet who forcibly aspects Luna and Mercury with a partial aspect.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Essential Dignities
text: If none have a partial aspect to Mercury or Luna, then he shall signify the manners, who in the place of Mercury and Luna hath the most essential dignities.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Fixed Stars
text: The Significator of Manners joined to fixed Stars of the first or second magnitude, being but a little distant from the Ecliptic, have great signification in the Manners, and make those signified to be more apparent; for if the Significator of Manners be with Caput Medusæ in 2h, by it it begets in the Native a certain dogged nature and violence, whereby he either causes sudden death unto himself, or is the cause of it to others. The Pleiades in 24, inclines the Native to be wanton, ambitious, turbulent. Oculus % in 4, to be fierce, full of courage to delight his own.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Saturn (Well Afflicted)
text: Strong and well affected, causes Grave persons, with a certain austerity, advised, excogitating profound matters, taciturn, solitary, laborious, patient, preservers of riches, sparing and thrifty, studious for their own profit, zealous, mistrustful.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Saturn (Weak & Unfortunate)
text: Weak and unfortunately posited, he shows Men of abject spirits, ill-favoured, having a low conceit of themselves, repiners, negligent, timorous, lovers of solitariness, sorrowful, envious, pertinacious, suspicious, backbiting, slanderous, superstitious, deceitful, malignant, rough-hewn fellows.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Jupiter (Well Dignified)
text: Well dignified and posited denotes Honest, religious, just, liberal, magnanimous, Governors, eminent men, performing high matters, sober, grave with a kind of moderation, prudent, living virtuously and orderly.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Jupiter (Ill Dignified)
text: When either ill dignified, ill posited and imperfect, it declares manners much of the nature before recited, but more obscure. Lovers of themselves, open-hearted innocent, a scornful, disdainful mind, proud, superstitious, fearful, dissembling, a kind of vain candour, negligent, prodigal.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Mars (Potent & Fortunate)
text: When potent and fortunate, he renders Generous men, valiant, full of courage, ireful, fierce and violent, apt with their hands, open in their speech, with a kind of temerity; fearing no bodily dangers, apt for government, boasters or crackers, aiming at revenge, impatient of servitude, or of receiving injuries or affronts.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Mars (Imbecile & Cadent)
text: When imbecile and cadent, or otherwise unfortunate, he declares Cruel man, quarrelsome and tyrannical, and head-strong, bloody minded, unashamed, sumptuous, braggers, impious, unjust, shedders of blood, impudent in provoking, but timorous when it comes to action, Thieves, authors of dissensions, tumults, sedition, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Venus (Well Constituted)
text: Well constituted in the Figure, and essentially strong, she intimates Pleasant, cheerful and fair conditioned men or persons, decent in their apparel, good, bountiful, merciful, prone to their delights, given to be cleanly, and to take pleasure in sports and pastimes, subtle, elegant, poetical.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Venus (Ill Placed)
text: Ill placed and weak, she demonstrates Fearful men, given to women, cowards, men of no spirits, sluggards, great Wooers of Ladies or women, lustful, not respecting their credit or esteem, zealous in women's matters, infamous, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Mercury (Well Disposed)
text: Well disposed in the Heavens, and in dignities, he foreshows Men of admirable sharp fancies, extreme studious and capable of learning, guileful or wily, wise, wary, divining well, or giving good advice, acting all things with agility and dexterity. Poets, Geometricians, Mathematicians, Astrologians, Eloquent, learning any Art, of good carriage or deportment.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners of Mercury (Ill Disposed)
text: Ill disposed, he predicts Unconstant people, malicious, turbulent, envious, perfidious, liars, to purpose, if he be with ¢, or in or aspect of h or ¢, if ¢, and in airy Signs, deceitful, inventing destructive plots and machinations, infamous, meddling with every body and every matter, asses, dolts, prattling dotards, stammering coxcombs, good for nothing, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Luminaries Influence on Manners
text: The posture of the Luminaries doth help their qualities; as thus, the ) with the principal Significator of manners, increasing in light, declares the Manners of the Native to manifest themselves, or causes the Native sooner to discover them; but when she is in ¢ or obscured, viz, either Combust or under the Sun beams, the Manners are not so manifest: in her greatest Septentrional or Meridional Latitude, she declares variety of Manners. The © being with the principal Significator of Manners, and he strong, causes a certain gravity of Manners, commixed with a kind of pleasantness or decency therein; but if © be weak, the Manners are less graceful, and show themselves but poorly, with no grace.

author: William Lilly
title: Dispositors of Luminaries Importance
text: Yet it is generally observed, that it is more material to consider the Dispositors of the Luminaries, than themselves, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Jupiter and Saturn Conjunction on Manners
text: If h be Significator of Manners and 2 be joined with him by body or aspect, then 2 qualifies the ill nature of h, and therefore you must not judge the Manners Saturnine, but to participate much of 2; and the Native shall be a very prudent, wise man, quiet, a man of great counsel and judgment, learned, &c. this is understood when 2 is pretty strong.

author: William Lilly
title: Mars and Saturn Conjunction on Manners
text: Let ¢ be associated with h, and let him be well fortified, it shows, the Native will begin to undertake any thing; but seldom conclude; for what the heat of ¢ stirs up, the coldness of h destroys again; the Native usually proves, a bragger, turbulent, seditious (fearfully bold), implacable, disdaining other men, vapouring, windy people, tyrannical, inhuman, given to all manner of villainy, dissembling both with God and man.

author: William Lilly
title: Saturn and Venus Conjunction on Manners
text: If h with the gentle Planet 9 is commixed, and he well fortified, he demonstrates a man little given to women, not ambitious, or delighting in pleasurable things or persons, delighted to be in the company of aged men, austere, envious, stiff in his own opinion, desirous to know the mysteries of nature, wary, suspicious in women's matters. If he be evil posited with 9, it shows an obscene companion; meddling or coveting so to do with any Woman, Kinswoman or other, one of no deliberation, a mere profane person.

author: William Lilly
title: Mars and Saturn Aspect on Manners
text: If ¢ be in aspect with h when he is Significator of Manners; and is well placed in the Heavens, the Native proves one of a curious understanding, greedy of Science and knowledge; one that will find out any Mystery; it notes people apt to medicine, admirable Architects, Sophisters, great Disputants, captions; discreet, sharp fancied, industrious, &c. Who desires to be satisfied further in the mixtures of the Planets, let them read Pontanus, de rebus Cœlestibus, lib. 6.

author: William Lilly
title: Manners from Infortunes
text: Observe notwithstanding, this general rule: That the worst manners are from the Infortunes, when joined to one another, or with the Sun in the 7th, 8th or 9th houses.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury's Influence on Wit and Capacity
text: Mercury gives the Capacity or Wit. By well mixing the significations, and if he have aspect unto him; for those Signs are most fertile. If ¥ is no way impeded, but posited in a good house of Heaven, Swift, Oriental, and with 2, gives a Wit capable of learning any thing; and usually men so endued, find out admirable Inventions. ¥ in either of his own houses, shows a sharp Understanding. ¥ received of the by house or Exaltation, gives a wonderful Fancy; generally good wits are produced, when most of the Planets are in airy Signs. ¥ in Y with reception by 2, gives a piercing Wit. ¥ with ) or 9, shows active spirits, prompt to any Science; best of all when she increases in light, and is not far from the full.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury's Position and Wit (1)
text: First, when he is under the earth and in no aspect with any Planet, he frames the mind more for Arts; when above the earth, he incites to Oratory.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury's Motion and Wit (2)
text: Secondly, when he is very swift in motion, he renders inconstant men, but quick of apprehension; oft changing their opinions, but yet will give good reason for their opinions: if he be Retrograde or slow, he argues stuttering companions, men of no conceptions.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury Combust and Wit (3)
text: Thirdly, when he is Combust, or under the Sun-beams; he incites the Wit to meddle with impertinent matters, or mere niceties.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury Oriental/Occidental and Wit (4)
text: Fourthly, when Oriental he expresses a more liberal nature; Occidental, a dissembler: but you must observe, that the Planet who governs the place where ¥ is, hath great force in directing the aforesaid decrees; that is, if the Planet is good, he changes and varies the nature of ¥ to good; if ill, he makes him a worse.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury in Airy Signs and Wit (5)
text: Fifthly, when he is not afflicted especially of ¢, but is well placed, and in an airy Sign, especially ¤, and with 2 or @, he renders most acute and witty men, and good Linguists speaking many Languages.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury in Angle and Wit (6)
text: Sixthly, ¥ being in any angle, especially in the ascendant, and in one of his own houses, and in any Sign but % and tL, he shows a Wit apt and fit for any employment.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury on Ascendant Cusp and Wit (7)
text: Seventhly, where ¥ is found upon the cusp of the ascendant in an airy Sign, and is also swift, it prenotes a good memory and understanding, but the person mutable.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury Conjunctions and Wit
text: Generally, ¥ with h makes the wit more wary, and the man more constant and persevering. ¥ with 2 more honest, learned and of upright judgment. ¥ with ¢ more confident and presumptuous. With the © ambitious, arrogant and proud. With 9 more eloquent and lovesom. With the ) more unstable. Again, h helps the memory; 2 introduces honesty and humanity, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Signs of Corrupt Understanding
text: ¥ Peregrine, Cadent in house, Combust, slow in motion, afflicted partially by the Infortunes, especially of ¢, causes a corrupt Wit, and a doltish Understanding; the more ¥ is afflicted, the greater misfortune happens in the Wit and Fancy: also ¥ separated from the ), and in no aspect with her, declares a weak Capacity. ¥ under the © beams, and also Retrograde, causes such to be very slow in their actions, and but of dull invention; ¥ in watery Signs, usually without the aspect of one of the Fortunes, shows an Idiot; and if in those Signs ¢ afflict him, the Native stutters, or hath an impediment in his speech. Predominant ¥ in ¢ or 7, or in his ¢, declares an untoward Wit, evil and malicious.

author: William Lilly
title: Temperaments and Study
text: Observe, Phlegmatic Signs are enemies to study, and show slowness: an overplus of Melancholy declares very asses: men of Sanguine temperature seldom are permanent in studies, nor doth the mere Choleric man much love his study. A Sanguine-melancholy man, makes the best Student: Choleric-melancholic men have excellent inventions.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Planet Aspecting Ascendant Lord
text: The Stature of the Body principally is adjudged tall or low from that Planet who doth partially behold the Lord of the ascendant; if many do behold him, then judge from the strongest.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Jupiter's Position
text: Oriental 2 prenotes A moderate stature declining rather to brevity. Occidental 2 A short stature. Oriental 3 A goodly tall stature. Occidental 3 Of moderate stature, but inclining to tallness.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Saturn's Position
text: Oriental h makes Tall Stature. Occidental h Moderate in height, but more long than short.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Sun's Position
text: Oriental © One more tall and slender. Occidental © Of more short stature, inclining to brevity.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Venus's Position
text: Oriental 9 Of middle stature, but verging to length. Occidental 9 Of middle stature, or but moderately tall.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Mercury's Dispositor
text: Yet it is very observable, that ¥, whether he be Oriental or Occidental, doth form the body according to the nature of his Dispositor; and if he be constituted either in his own house or the ©'s, or in the ) her house, he moderates the stature of the body according to the nature of the Signe.

author: William Lilly
title: Factors for Body Form and Shape
text: For describing the Form and Shape of the Body, consider the Sign ascending and his Lord, the Planet or Planets in the ascendant, or aspecting it, the two Lights, viz. © and ), the season of the year, and the fixed Stars in the ascendant, or near the cusp thereof.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Signs
text: Y S 3 I Discern a moderate Stature, but more long. ¤ & A Body more tall. Õ A Short. Õ Indifferent. Õ A moderate proportion.

author: William Lilly
title: Factors for Face and Hair Color
text: First, it is judged by the Planets in the ascendant of whose colours we have delivered our opinion in the first Part of this Work, from pag. 57. to 83. Secondly, from the Sign ascending and intercepted, if any be. Thirdly, from the Lords thereof. Fourthly, from the Planet or Planets partially beholding the degree ascending, or Lord of the ascendant. Fifthly, from fixed Stars arising with the ascendant, and which do preserve the colours of those Planets whose natures they participate with. Sixthly, from the temperament; for Sanguine complexions are fair or clear; Phlegmatic are pale; Choleric are yellow or red; Melancholic are black. Where note, the presence of one or both the Fortunes in the ascendant, give a good and graceful colour, the Infortunes both an evil one, and usually uncomely.

author: William Lilly
title: Signs and Color
text: Septentrional Signs, as s 1S QM, so also x and a, show a cheerful colour: Winter Signs, or the houses of the Infortunes, show a Countenance more sad; the colours notwithstanding are only White, Black, Yellow, Red, all the rest come by mixture of these: but to judge of the colour, do thus: Assign to every Significator his several colour, after, collect the sum into one, according to the greater number so judge, having judiciously framed a right mixture, consideration being also had to the Climate or Country where the Native is born; for though in your collection of testimonies, you may find the signification of a fair person, yet if he be a Spaniard your judgment will fail, for they are usually swarthy or black; the Danes are fair or red-haired, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Factors for Face and Members Proportion
text: Five things are considerable in the proportion of the Face and members, viz. the Sign ascending and his Lord, the Planets, or the configurations they have in the ascendant, the © and ), the quarter of the year, and fixed Stars in the ascendant.

author: William Lilly
title: Signs and Complexions
text: Humane Signs ascending, as IT ®, and the first part of 2 and sz, show fair and clear Complexions. s @ tL vw and x, show deformity, so the latter part of Y. 2 and 9 of all the Planets, give the best Complexions; 8 and ) the next: yet if they be evil Planets, they show ill Faces or Complexions, but when no way afflicted, they declare a good and handsome Face: h and ¢ and their unlucky configuration to the ascendant, show unhandsomeness: an Infortune in the ascendant, viz. h or ¢, a Scar or blemish in the Face.

author: William Lilly
title: Luminaries and Face/Eyes
text: © and ) well dignified, show fairness, yet © gives proportion not pulchritude. Where the Lights are both impeded, there’s some hurt in the Eyes, when the Infortunes are joined together, or in conjunction with the Lights in ¢ or @, or with their own h or ¢, or have no latitude, or are in their extremest latitude, they deform the Body by crookedness, lameness, King's evil, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Seasonal Signs and Body Form
text: Again, Vernal Signs show a fair form or shape, fleshy, a loveliness both of hair and colour, and a Sanguine Complexion. Estival Signs declare the members more gross, tending to middle stature, much Hair, great Eyes, and a choleric Complexion. Autumnal Signs argues lean bodies, Hairs extended abroad, fair Eyes, of decent stature, of a Melancholic Complexion. Hibernal Signs demonstrate a decent form of the Native, black, swart colour, the Hair spreading abroad, and but thin, a Phlegmatic constitution.

author: William Lilly
title: Fixed Stars and Pulchritude
text: The fixed Stars assist in pulchritude or deformity, according to the nature of that Planet whose condition they assimilate.

author: William Lilly
title: Jupiter/Venus Dignity and Stature
text: Either 2 or 9 being in their Houses or Exaltations; beholding the ascendant, do argue a tall stature; the contrary: when they are in their Falls or Detriment.

author: William Lilly
title: Mars/Saturn Affliction and Stature
text: 2 or ¢ in the Fall, Detriment or Retrograde, do declare a middle stature, yet tending to brevity: but if they be in their Falls or Detriment, and not Retrograde, they vary not the stature.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Lord of Ascendant (No Partial Aspect)
text: If no Planet do partially behold the Lord of the ascendant, then judgment must be derived from the Lord of the ascendant, the Sign he is in not considered, if he be direct.

author: William Lilly
title: Stature from Retrograde Lord of Ascendant
text: If he be Retrograde and in his Fall, then we judge not of the stature according to the nature of the Planet, but Sign where in he is: After the same manner the Luminaries having power and dignity in the Horoscopes, do discover the stature according to the quality of the Sign which they possess; but ¢ having dominion in the ascendant, gives the stature according to the nature of the Planet who is his Dispositor, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Grosness or Leanness of Bodies
text: We call that the grossness or leanness of Bodies, which befalleth by nature to bodies after a full age, or about thirty years, or somewhat after. The judgment hereof is assumed from the Sign ascending and his Lord, viz, from that Planet who hath most dignities therein.

author: William Lilly
title: Signs and Body Grossness/Leanness
text: Y S ¤ first part declare grossness, the latter part leanness. tL M 1, the first part of the Signs leanness, the latter grossness. % W, first part mediocrity, rather lean, the latter part tending more to grossness. y tty the first part lean, the latter part grossness. 2 MR, shows a moderate proportion of Bodies; but the latter part of x declines to leanness.

author: William Lilly
title: Lord of Ascendant and Body Grossness/Leanness
text: The Lord of the ascendant is thus considered, if he behold the degree ascending partially, take your judgment according to the nature of the Sign ascending: if it be not so, then refer to the Lord of the ascendant.

author: William Lilly
title: Judging Direction's Nature
text: In the first place therefore, we must consider whether the Direction we would judge of, be good or evill; which is knowne easily by the good or evill aspects.

author: William Lilly
title: Effects of Fortunate Directions
text: Secondly, a good Direction and a fortunate one, gives Prosperity and Fortune, such as the Significator of the Direction doth signifie: and intimate of himselfe, shall come to passe in such or such things, or from such or such persons; which he signifies by Accident, viz. by reason of the house in which he is constituted, or of what house he is Lord of in the Root of the Nativity.

author: William Lilly
title: Cause of Felicity in Directions
text: Thirdly, the cause of the Felicity is declared from the Promittor, according to the same manner as is mentioned of the Significator, by himselfe and by Accident; afterwards from the house of Heaven wherein the Direction doth meet, viz. in what house the degree of the Promitter doth happen.

author: William Lilly
title: Effects of Evil Directions
text: Fourthly, an evill Direction portends adverse Fortune in such things which the Significator of himselfe and by Accident doth signifie.

author: William Lilly
title: Cause of Infelicity in Directions
text: Fiftly, the originall cause of the Infelicity shall arise from the Men and Matters which the Promittor of that Direction doth signifie of himselfe and by Accident, consideration had to the house in which the occurse doth happen.

author: William Lilly
title: Ascendant Directional Significance
text: First, the Horoscope, or ascendant, we direct in every Nativity, for that it signifieth the Life and Body of man, his Complexion, the Affections and Manners of his Body and Mind: If he come to benevolent Directions, he signifieth prosperity both of Body and Mind, the Native’s Health, Cheerfulnesse, and all manner of earthly Contents: But if the ascendant have progresse to contrary Directions, viz. to the Termes or unfortunate aspects of the malevolent Planets, or accidentally unto the evil aspects of the Lords of the sixth, twelfth, and eighth houses, then evill is to be expected.

author: William Lilly
title: Moon Directional Significance
text: Secondly, we direct the ), in regard she signifies the Complexion of the Body, and its Intentions, the Natives Journeys, Peregrinations, his Matrimony, the state of his Wife, Women and neer Kinsfolkes.

author: William Lilly
title: Sun Directional Significance
text: Thirdly, the Directions of the © are made especially, concerning the Native’s good or bad Health, his Honour or Preferment publick or private, the favour of great Persons, the state of his Father, and his Estimation.

author: William Lilly
title: Midheaven Directional Significance
text: Fourthly, medium-celi we direct for Honour, Offices in the Common-wealth, the friendship of Nobility, Kings and Magistrates, for the Magistery, Trade or Profession of the Native, and his Mother.

author: William Lilly
title: Part of Fortune Directional Significance
text: Fiftly, © being directed to the good or evill aspects of the Fortunes or Infortunes shewes the encrease or diminution of Riches: And there are some will have that its Directions doe signifie the affects of the Body; of which I have no belief; the same thing the Dispositor of the © shall signifie: It may be directed according to succession of Signes, and contrary, but the best Practisers doe it onely according to succession.

author: William Lilly
title: Saturn Directional Significance
text: Sixtly, you may direct b to signifie your Ancestors, Inheritances, Buildings, Possessions, the Fruits of the earth; so also, to Fears, Jealousies, Mistrusts, &c. according as he is well or ill affected.

author: William Lilly
title: Jupiter Directional Significance
text: Seventhly, we direct ¥ for Glory, Renowne, Riches, Children, Religion, Sobriety, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Mars Directional Significance
text: Eighthly, ¢ is directed for Animofity, Victory, War, Lawsuits, and he shewes the estate of Brethren.

author: William Lilly
title: Venus Directional Significance
text: Ninthly, 9 is directed for Matrimony, Love, Pleasure, rich Ornaments, Maids, Women, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Mercury Directional Significance
text: Tenthly, we direct § for the Wit, Understanding, Trade, Industry, Negotions, Journeys, our lesser Brethren, for Scholarship, History, &c.

author: William Lilly
title: Planetary Signification in Directions
text: The Planets do signifie these things properly of themselves in Directions, in what Nativity soever they be, or in what part of Heaven; but accidentally, they have signification according to the nature of the Houses they are in, and are Lords of: by considering whereof, you shall find the true intention of what is signified by the Direction.

author: William Lilly
title: Directional Effects by Significator/Promittor Strength
text: The vehemency & greatnes of the effects of every Direction is forced & taken from the strength both of the Significator and Promittor in the Root of the Nativity: for when both of them are in their Essentiall Dignities, and in angles, the effects of their Direction shall be evident and manifest, and performe fully what was promised by it: as if the © were directed to the Radix, for the * of the ), both of them in their Essentiall Dignities; without doubt upon such a Direction, the Native would attain admirable Felicity when ever the Significator and Promittor did meet.

author: William Lilly
title: Directional Effects with Unequal Strength
text: If the one of them is powerfull, the other weak, the effects will be meane: and you must observe whether the Significator or Promittor be better dignified; if the Significator be more strong, the effects of the Happinesse shall be without any great or weighty cause to procure it: but if the Promittor be most fortified, the Felicity verily shall appeare, but not according to the magnitude of the cause from whence the Felicity had its original.

author: William Lilly
title: Directional Effects with Mutual Weakness
text: When both of them are weak in the Radix, the effects of the Direction will prove accordingly, and the occasion of the Happinesse promised as poore; by how much both Significator and Promittor are more weak, by so much shall the Events be lesse answerable, and the Causes from whence they should proceede.

author: William Lilly
title: Directional Effects with Total Weakness
text: If both of them be wholly weak and unfortunate, the effects of the Direction may come to nothing, and onely a shadow thereof appeare, but nothing effected.

author: William Lilly
title: Strong Infortunes as Promittors
text: You must also observe, that if the Infortunes, being Promittors in any Directions, doe portend an Infelicity, or any sad Events, the evils will be more tolerable, and doe lesse mischiefe: if in the Radix they were strong, or posited in their owne houses, for they hurt not their owne houses or overthrow their owne significations, and are wont then to doe lesse evill when well affected, then otherwise.

author: William Lilly
title: Conflicting Directions Resolution
text: It sometimes happens, that in the same yeer of the Native, there are good and ill directions of divers significations, so that the Astrologer may seem doubtfull what to doe in that case; yet in my judgment there can be no ambiguity therein, for both the Directions may exercise their effects in the same yeer without mutuall disturbance of one another, unlesse there be a certaine agreement or conveniency of them; as of the ) and Horoscopes whereof both of them signifie the Health of Body; for let us admit that the ascendant in the same yeer comes to the Quadrature of ¢ in a fiery Signe, arguing thereby a violent Feaver unto the Native, &c. let us suppose the ) in the same yeer meets with the * or A of ¢ in a watry Signe, the Quere is, What judgment shall be given herein? viz. Whether the Native shall be sick, or escape?
